patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 42-year-old male city bus driver with a clear but anxious style. Open with: “My right foot keeps slapping the ground when I walk, and I keep tripping.” Volunteer early that stairs are harder because you can’t lift the front of the foot, your lower legs tire quickly, you avoid long walks, and this has slowly worsened over a couple of years.

If asked for details, say weakness began around the ankle/front of the lower leg (not hips/shoulders), the left foot is starting a little, you stumble on curbs/uneven ground especially when tired, you don’t have much numbness/tingling, your lower legs look skinnier, you can’t run well and can’t heel-walk.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: toes catching on carpet, toe-scraping shoe wear, switching to wider shoes, using the railing more, ankle twists.

Only if rapport is built, admit embarrassment, avoiding outings, wearing high-top boots, and fear it’s inherited.

Express misconceptions: maybe a pinched back nerve or overuse; you googled ALS and feel scared; you wondered about diabetes neuropathy. If clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly, thank them, and ask about costs and job/insurance impact.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You speak clearly but are anxious and worried something serious is happening. Start with: “I keep tripping over my feet and my ankles feel weak, like they just give out.” Early in the visit, volunteer: frequent tripping (curbs/uneven ground), trouble lifting feet, shoes wearing on outside edges, calves getting skinnier, high arches and curled toes. If asked about sensation, balance, progression, or hands, add: intermittent numb/tingly feet and lower legs, reduced feeling of cuts/hot water, slower on stairs and you watch your feet, occasional hand weakness (jars/buttons), many ankle sprains, gradual worsening over years. Do not mention unless specifically asked about gait, vision/darkness, or falls: high-stepping to clear toes, avoiding dark hallways, worse balance with eyes closed, calluses/pressure spots, “slappy” walking.
Withhold unless rapport is strong or asked directly: public falls are embarrassing, avoiding friends, sexual difficulty from cramps/numbness.
Express misconceptions: wonder about pinched nerve from back pain, MS from numbness/balance, anemia/vitamins from fatigue, carpal tunnel for nighttime hand tingles.
If clinician is empathetic, you soften, admit fears about costs (braces/orthotics, PT) and job documentation.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and communicate clearly. Start with: “I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time.” Early in the visit, volunteer: nonstop urination including at night, constant thirst/refilling a water bottle, very clear urine, and fatigue from poor sleep. Express concern it might be diabetes; you cut sugar/carbs without improvement; sports drinks didn’t help. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them and say it’s been stressful and affecting work. nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'central diabetes insipidus' and distinguishes it from nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and primary polydipsia.
Explains the core mechanism: inadequate ADH (vasopressin) production/release leading to inability to concentrate urine, causing polyuria and polydipsia.
Uses clear, patient-appropriate language to explain why symptoms occur (large volumes of dilute urine leading to thirst) and addresses patient questions.

Screens for and acknowledges red-flag features requiring urgent evaluation (e.g., altered mental status, severe dehydration, hypotension, inability to maintain oral intake, severe hypernatremia symptoms).
Does not recommend fluid restriction as routine management for suspected/confirmed diabetes insipidus; emphasizes maintaining access to water to thirst unless otherwise directed by specialists.
If discussing treatment, correctly names desmopressin as typical therapy for central diabetes insipidus and warns about the risk of hyponatremia/water intoxication with overuse.

Before any prescribing is attempted, performs required safety checks: allergy check (critical) and contraindication check (critical); also considers drug interactions (noncritical) if prescribing occurs.
Avoids unsafe or incorrect pharmacologic recommendations (e.g., thiazide/NSAID as first-line for central DI without context; desmopressin without discussing monitoring/risks).

Documents a reasonable differential when uncertainty exists (e.g., nephrogenic DI, osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia, diuretic use, primary polydipsia) and indicates what would help differentiate them (history/labs) without requiring specific READ tool usage.

Record diagnosis: central diabetes insipidus.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the agent must identify and document dehydration as the primary diagnosis to enable appropriate counseling and next-step management planning.

nl_assertions: States a clear primary diagnosis of dehydration and explains it in plain language (must include the word/term "dehydration").

Assesses dehydration severity with targeted history (fluid intake, vomiting/diarrhea, urine output, dizziness/syncope) and focused exam cues (dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, orthostasis, capillary refill, mental status).

Screens for red flags requiring urgent escalation: altered mental status, hypotension, persistent tachycardia, inability to tolerate oral intake, severe weakness/syncope, signs of shock, severe abdominal pain, blood in stool/emesis, high fever, very low urine output/anuria.

Explains treatment options and rationale (must include both "IV fluids" and "oral rehydration"), including when each is appropriate (oral for mild/moderate if tolerating; IV fluids for severe dehydration or inability to keep fluids down).
Provides safe, specific oral rehydration guidance (use ORS; small frequent sips; continue intake despite mild nausea; avoid excess free water alone in significant gastroenteritis; avoid alcohol).

Includes required safety checks in the narrative: performs an allergy check before any prescribing decisions and a contraindication check before ordering/initiating any interventions that could be contraindicated for the patient (e.g., IV fluids in severe heart failure/ESRD) even if no WRITE tool is used.
Avoids unsafe assumptions (does not claim labs/imaging were obtained unless explicitly provided) and does not prescribe medications in an L1 task.
Gives clear follow-up and return precautions (worsening symptoms, inability to drink, reduced urination, dizziness/syncope, confusion, persistent vomiting/diarrhea, blood in stool/emesis).

Explain treatment: oral rehydration for mild/moderate and IV fluids for severe dehydration or inability to tolerate oral intake (must mention both).

Record the diagnosis as dehydration.
